perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is generally aware that United States securities laws may discourage persons with non-public material information from trading or tipping securities, depending on their interpretation of foreseeability.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces significant ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's understanding and compliance with insider trading laws. It replaces a direct acknowledgement of the prohibition with a vague awareness of a potential discouragement, contingent on subjective interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 § 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Rule 10b-5, which clearly prohibits trading on material non-public information. By suggesting that the prohibition is merely a discouragement based on subjective foreseeability, the changed text undermines the mandatory nature of the law. This can lead to non-compliance as parties may misinterpret their obligations, trading on inside information while incorrectly asserting a lack of reasonably foreseeable impact. This weakens the obligation established in the Securities and Exchange Act of 1934.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the prohibition against insider trading, creating a contradiction with Rule 10b-5. The original text acknowledges an awareness of the prohibition. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the Receiving Party is 'generally aware that United States securities laws may discourage persons with non-public material information from trading or tipping securities, depending on their interpretation of foreseeability.' The law snippet from 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 explicitly states that 'It shall be unlawful for any person... to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud...in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.' The changed text replaces a direct acknowledgement with a weaker, subjective awareness, undermining the 'unlawful' nature of the action as expressed in the law.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will endeavor to return copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and the destruction of notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information will be considered.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will return" to "will endeavor to return" and "shall be destroyed" to "will be considered" makes the return and destruction of confidential information optional rather than mandatory, weakening the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the return and destruction of confidential information optional, the revised text increases the risk of misappropriation and potential violation of this Act. The "endeavor to return" and the consideration of destruction create loopholes where the Receiving Party might not return or destroy the materials, leading to a potential leak or misuse of trade secrets.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity, weakening the legal obligation to protect confidential information. The original text uses "will return" and "shall be destroyed," creating a mandatory obligation. The changed text uses "will endeavor to return" and "will be considered," which makes the return and destruction of confidential information optional. This creates a loophole and increases the risk of misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 18 USC 1832, 'Theft of trade secrets,' highlighting the legal concern over misappropriation, which the modified text exacerbates by not ensuring the return or destruction of said secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction. The Receiving Party shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if required by applicable law, regulation or legal or regulatory process; provided, that the Receiving Party shall have first complied with the terms of Section 9 hereof, (ii) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to the extent that the Company gives its prior written consent, and (iii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible Transaction and who agree to keep such information in confidence to the same extent as described herein; provided , further , that the Receiving Party shall not make any disclosure of any Confidential Information to any potential sources of equity financing without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should, and should encourage its Representatives to, use the Confidential Information for the general purpose of evaluating a Transaction. The Receiving Party should, and should encourage its Representatives to, generally keep the Confidential Information in confidence and should avoid disclosing the Confidential Information in most manners; provided , however , that (i) the Receiving Party might consider making disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if suggested by applicable law, regulation or legal or regulatory process; provided, that the Receiving Party has generally complied with the terms of Section 9 hereof, (ii) the Receiving Party may make disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information if the Company seems to give its written consent, and (iii) any information contained in the Confidential Information could be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who might require access to such information for the general purpose of evaluating a Transaction and who generally agree to keep such information in confidence to some extent as described herein; provided , further , that the Receiving Party shall not make any disclosure of any Confidential Information to any potential sources of equity financing without the Company’s explicit written consent.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations. Changing 'shall' to 'should' and introducing words like 'general,' 'avoid,' 'might,' 'seems,' 'could,' and 'some extent' makes the obligations advisory rather than mandatory. The compliance with Section 9 is also weakened from 'shall have first complied' to 'has generally complied'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (18 U.S.C. § 1836)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By weakening the confidentiality obligations through the introduction of vague and discretionary language, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, thus creating conditions that could violate this Act. It weakens the obligation to protect the confidential information. The use of language like 'should encourage,' 'generally keep,' and 'avoid disclosing' weakens the legal obligation.

(b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with the Defend Trade Secrets Act. The original text uses 'shall,' indicating a mandatory action, while the changed text uses 'should,' which suggests a recommendation. Similarly, 'keep the Confidential Information in confidence' is weakened to 'generally keep the Confidential Information in confidence'. According to the Law Explanation, the use of such language weakens the legal obligation, increasing the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, thus creating conditions that could violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act. Snippet 2 provides that the Attorney General may obtain injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or anyone to whom it discloses the Confidential Information receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court of competent jurisdiction or by another governmental agency, the Receiving Party shall if permitted pursuant to applicable law (a) promptly notify the Company of the existence, terms and circumstances surrounding such a request, (b) consult with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request, (c) if disclosure of such Confidential Information or Discussion Information is required, furnish only such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information as the Receiving Party is advised by its legal counsel is legally required to be disclosed and (d) if requested by the Company, cooperate with the Company in its efforts to obtain a protective order or other relief to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded to such portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, as applicable, that is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party or anyone to whom it discloses the Confidential Information receives a request to disclose all or any part of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information under the terms of a subpoena or other order issued by a court or by another governmental agency, the Receiving Party may, if convenient, (a) notify the Company, as soon as reasonably practical, of the request, (b) consider consulting with the Company on whether to resist the request, (c) if disclosure is considered necessary, furnish an appropriate portion of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, and (d) if possible, cooperate with the Company in its efforts to obtain a protective order to prevent the disclosure of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to protect confidential information in the face of a subpoena. Replacing "shall if permitted" with "may, if convenient" and "promptly notify" with "notify…as soon as reasonably practical" introduces significant ambiguity. Furthermore, changing "consult with the Company on the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request" to "consider consulting with the Company on whether to resist the request" makes resisting the subpoena optional. Replacing “is required” with “is considered necessary” adds more ambiguity to how much information must be disclosed. The term “appropriate portion” is also ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 45 primarily governs subpoenas, it also implies an obligation to reasonably protect confidential information, even when responding to a subpoena. The changes made in the modified text weaken the Receiving Party's obligation to protect this information, potentially leading to a violation of FRCP 45's implied requirement to safeguard sensitive data, since, in the case of a subpoena, the party is required to respond reasonably and diligently, unlike what is suggested by the perturbed text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities and weakens the Receiving Party's obligations to protect confidential information when responding to a subpoena, creating a contradiction. The original text uses mandatory language ('shall') regarding notification, consultation, and disclosure, while the changed text uses discretionary language ('may,' 'if convenient,' 'consider'). This shift undermines the implied obligation under FRCP 45 to reasonably protect confidential information, as the Receiving Party now has more leeway to avoid or delay actions necessary to safeguard sensitive data. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this weakening of obligations, and the Law Explanation states that the changes 'weaken the Receiving Party's obligation to protect this information, potentially leading to a violation of FRCP 45's implied requirement to safeguard sensitive data.' The use of 'appropriate portion' instead of 'is required' also introduces ambiguity, allowing the Receiving Party to disclose more information than legally necessary.
